title: 20-Meter Circle Precision
steps: Set up four cones in a diamond pattern at 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock positions, each 10 meters from the center of your circle.
At the walk, ride a circle touching each cone. Focus on keeping an even bend through your horse's entire body, from poll to tail.
Check that your inside leg stays at the girth, your outside leg slightly behind the girth, and your inside rein asks for gentle flexion while the outside rein supports.
Ride 4-5 circles at the walk, then pick up a working trot and ride 4-5 more circles. Maintain the same track.
Practice seeing the next cone ahead and planning your curve. Your horse's barrel should be bending around your inside leg.
Change direction across the diagonal and repeat on the other rein. Compare the quality and ease of each direction.
Finish by removing the cones and riding the same circle from memory, checking if you maintain the same size and shape.
mistakes: Letting the circle shrink to less than 20 meters, especially in the corners near the rail.
Pulling on the inside rein instead of using inside leg to create bend.
Looking down at the horse instead of looking ahead to the next quarter of the circle.
Losing rhythm or tempo as you navigate the circle.
variations: Try 15-meter circles for an added challenge, requiring more bend and balance from your horse.
Add transitions within the circle: walk to trot and back to walk, maintaining the same track.
Ride a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles, changing bend smoothly through X.
tips: Tip: Film yourself from above if possible. Seeing your track from a bird's-eye view reveals asymmetries you can't feel from the saddle.

title: 15-Meter Circle at the Trot
steps: Place four cones marking the quarter points of a 15-meter circle at E or B.
Pick up a working trot and begin riding the circle, aiming to pass directly over each cone.
Keep your inside leg at the girth to maintain bend. Your outside leg sits just behind the girth to prevent the haunches from swinging out.
Use your outside rein as a supporting rein to control the size of the circle. Avoid over-bending through the neck.
Ride 5 complete circles, then transition to walk and check your horse for straightness on a straight line.
Change direction and repeat on the other rein. Note which direction feels easier for your horse.
Progress by riding the circle without cones, then checking your track in the footing.
mistakes: Making an egg shape instead of a true circle because the horse drifts toward the gate.
Over-bending the neck while the shoulder falls out, creating a bulging circle.
Sitting crooked with more weight in the outside stirrup.
Rushing the tempo as the circle size decreases from 20 to 15 meters.
variations: Shrink to a 12-meter circle for 2 revolutions, then leg yield back out to 15 meters.
Add halt transitions on the circle, maintaining the curved track.
Alternate between 15-meter and 20-meter circles at the same arena letter.
tips: Tip: Think of your circle as a clock face. At each quarter point, briefly check: is my horse's body uniformly bent from poll to tail?

title: 10-Meter Circle with Cone Guides
steps: Set up four cones in a 10-meter diameter circle at C or A. The cones should be 5 meters from center.
Begin at the walk, riding the tight circle and focusing on maintaining three-track bend throughout.
Your inside leg drives at the girth. Your outside leg controls the haunches. The outside rein limits neck bend while the inside rein suggests flexion.
After 3-4 walk circles, pick up sitting trot. The smaller circle demands more engagement from the hind legs.
Maintain a steady rhythm. If your horse falls onto the forehand, half-halt and re-balance before continuing.
Ride 3 circles in each direction at the trot, then straighten onto a long side to assess whether your horse steps more actively behind.
Cool down with a 20-meter circle at the walk to stretch your horse's topline after the collected work.
mistakes: Allowing the circle to grow larger than 10 meters because the horse resists the bend.
Over-collecting to the point where the horse loses impulsion and forward energy.
The rider tipping to the inside instead of staying centered over the horse.
Grinding around the circle without breaks, causing the horse to lean on the inside shoulder.
variations: Ride 10-meter circles at each letter (C, A, E, B) and compare the quality at each location.
Add a 10-meter circle to a canter transition: circle at trot, pick up canter on the open side.
Spiral in from 20 meters to 10 meters over 3-4 revolutions, then leg yield back out.
tips: Tip: Small circles are strength work for your horse. Limit to 3-4 revolutions before straightening to prevent soreness.

title: Spiral In and Out on a Circle
steps: Start on a 20-meter circle at the working trot. Establish a steady rhythm and consistent bend.
Over the next 2-3 revolutions, gradually spiral inward by applying more inside leg to outside rein, decreasing the circle by about 2 meters per revolution.
Continue spiraling until you reach approximately a 10-meter circle. Maintain the same tempo throughout.
Hold the 10-meter circle for one full revolution, then begin spiraling back out using a leg yield motion.
Push your horse outward with your inside leg while keeping the bend. The outside rein opens slightly to allow the expansion.
Return to the full 20-meter circle and check that your rhythm and balance have been maintained.
Repeat in the other direction. The stiff side will feel significantly harder; be patient and reward small improvements.
mistakes: Spiraling in too quickly, losing the gradual progression and creating a choppy circle.
Losing bend as you spiral out, causing the horse to fall through the outside shoulder.
Allowing the tempo to speed up on the spiral out or slow down on the spiral in.
Leaning in with your upper body instead of keeping your weight centered.
variations: Perform the spiral at the canter for advanced collection and balance work.
Add a transition to walk at the smallest circle, then trot on the spiral out.
Ride the spiral in posting trot, then switch to sitting trot for the spiral out.
tips: Tip: Count your revolutions on the way in so you can match them on the way out. Symmetry is the goal.

title: Circle at the Canter
steps: Begin at the working canter on a 20-meter circle. Establish a clear three-beat rhythm before focusing on accuracy.
Place four cones as quarter-point guides. Your horse should pass directly over each cone.
Sit deep in the saddle with your inside hip slightly forward. Your inside leg at the girth maintains impulsion while the outside leg behind the girth keeps the haunches on the track.
Focus on maintaining an even bend: you should see just the inside eye and nostril. The outside rein provides half-halts to balance the canter.
Ride 4-5 circles, then perform a downward transition to trot for 3-4 strides, and pick up canter again on the circle.
Change direction through trot, pick up the new lead, and repeat the circle work on the other rein.
Finish with a stretchy trot circle, allowing the horse to lower the neck and reach forward-downward.
mistakes: The horse breaking to trot because the rider is too tight with the reins or not supporting with the inside leg.
The canter becoming four-beat (lateral) due to the horse not being forward enough into the contact.
The circle drifting to one side because the rider doesn't correct with the outside aids.
Cross-cantering when picking up the new lead after a change of direction.
variations: Ride 15-meter canter circles requiring more collection and balance.
Add counter-canter on the circle for one revolution before returning to true canter.
Perform simple changes through walk at X when changing direction on a figure-eight.
tips: Tip: If the canter feels rushed, try a quiet 'and-one-two-three' count in your head to set the rhythm you want.

title: Figure-Eight Circles
steps: Set up a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles that meet at X. Place cones at the outer edges of each circle for reference.
Begin at the walk on the first circle. Ride one complete circle with proper bend.
As you cross through X, straighten your horse for 2-3 strides, then establish the new bend for the second circle.
Walk the entire figure-eight twice, focusing on the smooth change of bend through X.
Progress to the trot. The change of bend becomes more challenging at speed. Allow 3-4 strides of straightness at X.
Repeat the figure-eight 3-4 times at the trot. Each pass should improve the smoothness of your direction changes.
mistakes: Rushing the change of bend at X instead of allowing straight strides in between.
Making one circle larger than the other, creating a lopsided figure-eight.
The horse anticipating the direction change and cutting corners through X.
Losing rhythm at the crossover point where you change the bend.
variations: Ride the figure-eight at the canter with simple changes through trot or walk at X.
Use 15-meter circles instead of 20 for increased difficulty.
Add a halt at X between each circle to practice transitions and straightness.
tips: Tip: The quality of your figure-eight depends on the quality of your straight strides at X. Don't rush the changeover.

title: Volte at Walk
steps: A volte is a small circle of 6-8 meters. Place two cones 6 meters apart to define the diameter.
At the walk, ride a circle using the two cones as opposite points of the diameter.
Your horse needs significant bend for this small circle. Inside leg at the girth, outside leg behind the girth.
Keep the walk active and rhythmic. Small circles slow horses down if you don't maintain forward energy.
Ride 3-4 voltes, then straighten onto the track and walk forward on a long rein to stretch.
Repeat on the other rein. The volte will feel very different in each direction.
mistakes: Making the volte too large because the horse resists the tight bend.
The horse's haunches swinging out, making a pear shape instead of a circle.
Losing forward energy so the walk becomes lazy and shuffling.
Pulling the inside rein to force the bend instead of using the inside leg.
variations: Try the volte at the sitting trot once your horse maintains bend at the walk.
Ride a volte at each letter along the long side (H, S, E, V, K) as a series.
Use the volte as a preparation for lateral movements: volte then shoulder-in down the long side.
tips: Tip: Think of wrapping your horse around your inside leg like bending a flexible stick. The bend must be uniform from poll to tail.

title: Volte at the Trot
steps: Set up two cones 8 meters apart. You will ride a volte (small circle) using these as diameter points.
Pick up a sitting trot. The sitting trot gives you better control of the horse's body on a tight circle.
Begin the volte, keeping your inside leg firmly at the girth to create and maintain bend. Your outside leg guards the haunches.
Half-halt with the outside rein every quarter of the circle to prevent the horse from falling onto the inside shoulder.
Ride 3 voltes, then straighten out for 10-12 strides on the long side. This gives the horse a mental and physical break.
Return to the volte. On this second set, focus on keeping the hind legs stepping actively under the body.
Change direction and repeat. Keep sessions short since volte work is physically demanding.
mistakes: The horse falling onto the inside shoulder and leaning through the turn.
Sitting too heavily on one seat bone, throwing the horse off balance.
The volte becoming egg-shaped because the horse drifts at one point.
Keeping the horse on the volte too long, causing fatigue and resistance.
variations: Ride the volte at the canter (advanced) — this requires significant collection.
Chain voltes down the long side: volte at E, straight 5 strides, volte at B.
Exit the volte into shoulder-in for a seamless lateral movement transition.
tips: Tip: If your horse braces against the bend, ride one large circle first to establish the bend, then gradually decrease the size.

title: Tear-Drop Circle
steps: A tear-drop shape combines a straight line with a half-circle. Start at A or C, ride straight toward X.
At X, begin a 10-meter half-circle turning either left or right.
Complete the half-circle so you are now facing back the way you came, aligned with the center line.
Ride straight back down the center line to your starting letter.
The key is the transition from straight to curved: your horse should accept the bend smoothly without resistance.
Repeat the tear-drop 3 times in each direction, improving the accuracy of your turns each time.
Progress by performing the tear-drop at the trot, maintaining rhythm through the shape change.
mistakes: Starting the half-circle too early or too late, missing the X centerpoint.
Losing straightness on the center line portions.
The horse bulging through the outside shoulder on the curved portion.
Rushing through the shape instead of maintaining a steady tempo.
variations: Ride the tear-drop at the canter, using a downward transition for the half-circle.
Make the half-circle portion smaller (8 meters) for more collection demand.
Chain two tear-drops together: one left, one right, creating a continuous flowing pattern.
tips: Tip: The tear-drop is excellent for test preparation since many dressage tests require riding to X and turning.

title: Half-Circle and Return to Track
steps: Starting from the track, ride a half-circle of 10 meters into the arena, then ride a diagonal line back to the track.
Begin at a letter on the long side (like E or B). Turn off the track and ride the half-circle.
At the point farthest from the track, you should be 10 meters from the rail.
Now ride a straight diagonal line back to the track, arriving several meters ahead of where you started.
The diagonal portion should be ridden straight with the horse slightly positioned to the outside to prepare for the new bend on the track.
Practice this movement in both directions. Pay attention to the transition from curved to straight line.
mistakes: Making the half-circle too shallow, never truly reaching 10 meters from the track.
Losing the bend during the half-circle and cutting corners.
Not straightening the horse on the diagonal return line.
Arriving back at the track at an awkward angle instead of smoothly merging.
variations: Increase to a 15-meter half-circle for a shallower return angle.
Perform the exercise at the canter, including a lead change if crossing the center.
Add a transition to walk at the farthest point of the half-circle, then trot the return.
tips: Tip: This movement appears in many dressage tests. Practice it until the half-circle and return feel like one flowing movement.

title: Serpentine Three Loops
steps: Set up three equal loops spanning the width of the arena. Place cones at the center line to mark where each loop crosses.
Begin at A or C and ride the first loop across the full width of the arena, touching the opposite long side.
Cross the center line, straighten for 1-2 strides, then establish the new bend for loop two.
Each loop should be identical in size and shape, reaching the track on each side.
At the trot, the change of bend should be smooth. Allow your horse 2-3 straight strides at each center line crossing.
Ride the complete serpentine 3-4 times, focusing on equal-sized loops and clean bend changes.
mistakes: Making loops unequal in size, with the first or last loop much smaller.
Rushing the bend change at the center line instead of allowing straight strides.
Drifting forward or backward on each pass, so the serpentine migrates down the arena.
Losing the rhythm as you change direction.
variations: Increase to four or five loops for narrower, more demanding curves.
Ride the serpentine at the canter with simple changes at each center line crossing.
Add a transition to walk at each center line crossing before establishing the new bend.
tips: Tip: Each loop should touch the wall. If you find yourself short of the rail, your loops are too shallow.

title: Four-Loop Serpentine
steps: Divide the arena into four equal sections using cones along the center line.
Begin at A and ride the first loop to the right, reaching the track at K-E.
Cross the center line with 2 straight strides, then bend left for loop two, reaching the track at E-H.
Continue for all four loops, ensuring each is identical in depth and reaches the track.
The narrower loops (compared to 3-loop) demand quicker changes of bend and more lateral suppleness.
At the trot, focus on maintaining rhythm. The horse should not speed up or slow down at the direction changes.
Ride 3 complete passes, then walk on a long rein to reward your horse.
mistakes: Making the loops too shallow because of the narrower space.
The horse anticipating direction changes and cutting the loops short.
Inconsistent bend, with more bend in one direction than the other.
The rider's body getting behind the horse's motion at each bend change.
variations: Progress to a 5-loop serpentine for even tighter curves.
Ride the serpentine at the canter in counter-canter through the short loops.
Add 10-meter circles at the top of each loop before crossing back.
tips: Tip: Use the center line cones as your guide. Each loop should be geometrically equal — measure with your eye before you start.

title: Shallow Loop on the Long Side
steps: A shallow loop comes off the track 5 meters and returns. Place a cone 5 meters from the rail at the midpoint of the long side.
Track left along the long side. At the first quarter marker, begin a gentle curve inward toward the cone.
Pass the cone at the midpoint, 5 meters from the rail. The curve should be gradual, not a sharp turn.
After the cone, curve gently back to the rail, arriving at the third quarter marker of the long side.
The horse maintains the same bend throughout — you don't change the bend for this shallow loop.
Ride the shallow loop 3-4 times on each rein at the trot, focusing on a smooth, flowing arc.
mistakes: Coming too far off the track (more than 5 meters) or not far enough (less than 3 meters).
Changing the bend at the midpoint instead of maintaining the original bend.
The loop having a sharp angle at the departure and return points instead of smooth curves.
Looking at the cone instead of ahead to the return point on the rail.
variations: Increase the depth to 10 meters, requiring a change of bend at the midpoint.
Ride a shallow loop at the canter, which introduces counter-canter in the middle portion.
Chain shallow loops on both long sides for a continuous flowing exercise.
tips: Tip: Shallow loops are excellent for introducing counter-flexion and counter-canter in a gentle way.

title: Counter-Canter on a 20-Meter Circle
steps: Establish a balanced working canter on the true lead on a 20-meter circle.
Once balanced, ride out to the track and continue on the true lead past the short side.
As you reach the opposite 20-meter circle point, begin riding the circle in counter-canter (outside lead).
Maintain a very slight flexion to the lead — if on left lead, slight left flexion even though circling right.
Keep your lead-side leg at the girth and the other leg behind the girth, same as for true canter.
Ride 2-3 revolutions in counter-canter. If the horse tries to swap leads, half-halt and maintain your position.
Return to the original circle for true canter to reward balance. Walk and stretch.
mistakes: Losing the lead as the horse swaps behind or does a flying change.
Over-bending the neck toward the lead instead of keeping the horse straight.
The canter becoming four-beat due to tension and loss of impulsion.
The rider gripping with the knees and locking the hip, blocking the canter motion.
variations: Decrease the circle to 15 meters in counter-canter for advanced balance work.
Ride a full figure-eight maintaining the same lead throughout (true canter on one circle, counter on the other).
Add shallow loops in counter-canter along the long side.
tips: Tip: Think of carrying a glass of water. In counter-canter, smoothness matters more than accuracy. Don't sacrifice quality for geometry.

title: Change of Rein Across the Diagonal
steps: Start tracking left at the working trot. As you approach the corner before the long side, look across the diagonal to M (or K).
At the letter before the corner (F or H), begin turning across the diagonal toward the opposite corner letter.
Ride a straight diagonal line. Your horse should be perfectly straight — no bend in either direction.
As you approach the opposite track, prepare the new bend 2-3 strides before reaching the rail.
Smoothly join the track on the new rein with the correct bend already established.
Repeat 4-6 diagonal changes, alternating direction each time. Focus on the straightness of the diagonal line.
mistakes: The diagonal line curving instead of being perfectly straight.
Not preparing the new bend early enough, resulting in a clumsy arrival at the track.
The horse speeding up on the diagonal because it feels like open space.
Cutting the corner before the diagonal instead of riding deep into the corner.
variations: Perform the diagonal change at the canter, adding a simple change through trot at X.
Add a transition at X: trot to walk to trot, changing the posting diagonal.
Ride the diagonal in medium trot, collecting back to working trot at X.
tips: Tip: The diagonal is your horse's straightness test. If you drift to one side, it reveals which shoulder is dominant.

title: Diamond Pattern
steps: Set up four cones at the midpoints of each arena side: A, B, C, and E (or equivalent points).
Starting at A, ride a straight line to B. Turn 90 degrees and ride straight to C.
Turn 90 degrees at C and ride straight to E. Turn again and ride straight back to A.
This creates a diamond shape in the arena. Each straight segment should be truly straight.
The 90-degree turns test your horse's response to your aids. Prepare each turn 3-4 strides in advance.
Ride the diamond at the walk first, then progress to the trot. At the trot, the turns become half-halts.
Reverse direction and ride the diamond the other way to work both reins equally.
mistakes: The straight segments curving instead of being laser-straight.
Over-shooting or under-shooting the 90-degree turns at each cone.
Losing impulsion during the turns because of over-use of the reins.
The horse anticipating turns and cutting corners before reaching the cone.
variations: Ride the diamond at the canter with simple changes at each turn point.
Add a halt at each cone, stand for 3 seconds, then proceed to the next point.
Make a smaller diamond using cones placed closer to the center for tighter turns.
tips: Tip: Prepare your turns like a car approaching a corner: slow the body first, then turn the steering.

title: Cloverleaf Pattern
steps: Place one cone at X (the center of the arena). This is your base point for the cloverleaf.
Starting at X, ride a 10-meter circle to the right (first leaf). Return to X.
From X, ride a 10-meter circle forward (second leaf). Return to X.
From X, ride a 10-meter circle to the left (third leaf). Return to X.
From X, ride a 10-meter circle backward (fourth leaf). Return to X.
Each leaf requires a different bend. The transitions through X must be smooth and balanced.
Start at the walk, then progress to the trot. The pattern demands quick, accurate changes of direction.
mistakes: The leaves becoming different sizes instead of four equal 10-meter circles.
Losing track of which leaf comes next and getting disoriented.
The horse falling in or out at X where all the direction changes happen.
Not riding all the way back to X between leaves, cutting the transitions short.
variations: Make the leaves 15-meter circles for an easier but longer version.
Ride the cloverleaf at the canter with simple changes at X between each leaf.
Add transitions: trot the first leaf, walk through X, trot the second leaf, and so on.
tips: Tip: The cloverleaf is essentially four connected circles. Master each circle individually before combining them.

title: Circle Transitions: Walk-Trot-Walk
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle with cone markers at the quarter points.
Begin at the walk. As you pass the first cone, prepare to trot. At the second cone, pick up a working trot.
Trot for half the circle (from cone 2 to cone 4). At cone 4, prepare to walk. Transition to walk by cone 1.
Walk for half the circle, then trot again for the next half. This creates a rhythmic pattern.
Focus on making each transition smooth: the horse should flow from one gait to the next without jarring.
After 4-5 repetitions, change the pattern: trot for one quarter, walk for one quarter, alternating around the circle.
mistakes: The transitions being abrupt instead of smooth and flowing.
The horse breaking into trot before the designated cone or walking too late.
Losing the circle shape during transitions as the horse falls in or drifts out.
The rider throwing their body forward during the trot transition.
variations: Add canter transitions: walk-trot-canter-trot-walk around the circle.
Reduce the transition zones to specific cones (immediate transitions at the cone).
Perform the exercise on a 15-meter circle for more difficulty.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transitions 3-4 strides before the cone. The preparation is more important than the exact moment.

title: Canter Circle with Trot Transitions
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working canter on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to trot for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the canter again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: canter three-quarters, trot one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: trot three-quarters and canter only one-quarter of the circle.
The trot-to-canter transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced canter, then walk and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.
The trot section becoming rushed as the horse anticipates the canter depart.
Losing the circle shape during transitions.
The canter becoming flat and running after multiple transitions.
variations: Use walk transitions instead of trot for a more advanced collection exercise.
Reduce to 2-3 trot strides between canter segments for sharper transitions.
Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.
tips: Tip: If the horse picks up the wrong lead, calmly trot and try again. Don't punish, just redirect.

title: Stretchy Circle at the Trot
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Pick up a working trot with a steady contact.
Gradually allow the reins to slide through your fingers, giving your horse 2-3 inches of rein at a time.
Your horse should follow the contact downward, stretching the neck forward and down while maintaining the trot.
The circle should remain the same size and shape. The stretch should not cause the horse to fall onto the forehand or rush.
Maintain the stretch for 2-3 complete circles, keeping your leg on to support the rhythm.
Slowly take the reins back to working length, asking your horse to come back to the contact.
Repeat the give-and-retake 3-4 times. The stretch should improve with each repetition.
mistakes: Throwing the reins away all at once instead of gradually lengthening.
The horse falling onto the forehand and speeding up when given rein.
The circle growing larger as the horse loses balance during the stretch.
The rider rounding their back and collapsing the core when lengthening the reins.
variations: Try the stretchy circle at the canter for a more advanced relaxation exercise.
Add a few strides of medium trot on the stretchy circle to test throughness.
Alternate between collected circle and stretchy circle every 2 revolutions.
tips: Tip: The stretchy circle tests whether your horse is truly through and connected. A hollow horse will lift the head instead of stretching down.

title: Shoulder-In on the Rail
steps: Begin at the working trot, tracking right along the long side of the arena.
As you come out of the corner, maintain the bend you had in the corner but continue straight down the long side.
Your horse should be on three tracks: the inside hind leg stepping toward the line of the outside front leg.
Keep your inside leg at the girth to maintain impulsion and bend. Your outside leg behind the girth prevents the haunches from swinging out.
Your inside rein maintains flexion while the outside rein controls the degree of angle. Aim for about 30 degrees from the rail.
Ride shoulder-in for the length of the long side, then ride straight into the corner.
Repeat on the other rein. Most horses find shoulder-in easier on one side than the other.
mistakes: Too much neck bend without enough body angle, creating neck-in instead of shoulder-in.
Losing impulsion because the rider over-uses the reins to create the angle.
The angle varying along the long side instead of remaining consistent.
The horse drifting off the rail, turning instead of moving laterally.
variations: Ride shoulder-in at the walk first to establish the feel before adding trot.
Progress to shoulder-in at the collected trot for increased engagement.
Use shoulder-in as a preparation for half-pass: shoulder-in to half-pass mid-arena.
tips: Tip: Think of riding the horse's shoulders around your inside leg like wrapping a scarf. The bend comes from the body, not just the neck.

title: Leg Yield from the Quarter Line
steps: Place a cone at the quarter line (5 meters from the rail) at the start of the long side.
At the working trot, turn off the track onto the quarter line at the beginning of the long side.
Apply your inside leg at the girth to push the horse sideways toward the rail while maintaining forward movement.
Your horse should be mostly straight with just a slight flexion away from the direction of travel.
The horse should arrive at the rail by the end of the long side, covering both forward and sideways ground.
Walk and change direction. Repeat the leg yield from the other quarter line on the new rein.
mistakes: Too much neck bend, causing the horse to lead with the shoulder instead of moving evenly.
Losing forward impulsion — the horse slowing down as it moves sideways.
The horse not reaching the rail by the end of the long side (not enough sideways).
The rider collapsing their hip and leaning in the direction of travel.
variations: Start the leg yield from the center line to double the distance to the rail.
Add a transition to canter when you reach the rail to test straightness.
Ride leg yield in both directions without stopping: quarter line to rail, then rail to quarter line.
tips: Tip: Leg yield should feel like the horse is sliding sideways while walking forward. Think of it as walking diagonally.

title: Leg Yield Across the Diagonal
steps: Start tracking left at the working trot. At the letter before the corner, prepare to turn across the diagonal.
As you begin the diagonal line, apply your left leg to push the horse sideways to the right while maintaining forward motion.
The horse should be almost straight with a slight left flexion (flexion away from the direction of sideways travel).
Maintain an even tempo. The leg yield should not slow the horse down.
Aim to arrive at the opposite track with the horse stepping evenly both forward and sideways.
Walk, change direction, and repeat the leg yield on the other diagonal.
Practice until the leg yield feels smooth and the horse responds promptly to your lateral aid.
mistakes: The horse leading with the haunches instead of moving shoulders and haunches together.
Losing straightness through over-bending the neck.
Not maintaining enough forward energy, resulting in a sluggish sideways shuffle.
The angle of travel being too steep (too much sideways, not enough forward).
variations: Ride leg yield on the diagonal at the walk first for clearer aids.
Add a 10-meter circle at X before completing the leg yield to the rail.
Alternate leg yield direction: half-diagonal leg yield right, then half-diagonal leg yield left.
tips: Tip: Imagine your horse is moving through a narrow corridor angled across the arena. Forward must always be the priority over sideways.

title: Haunches-In (Travers)
steps: Begin at the collected trot, tracking right on the long side. Your horse should be straight and balanced.
As you leave the corner, keep the shoulders on the track but bring the haunches to the inside of the arena.
Your outside (right) leg behind the girth actively pushes the haunches inward. Your inside (left) leg at the girth maintains impulsion.
The horse should be on four tracks with the bend in the direction of travel — bent right while moving right.
Maintain approximately 35 degrees of angle from the rail. The horse looks in the direction it's traveling.
Ride haunches-in for the length of the long side, then straighten before the corner.
This is more difficult than shoulder-in because the horse bends toward the direction of movement.
mistakes: Too much angle causing the horse to lose impulsion and rhythm.
The horse tilting its head instead of bending uniformly through the body.
Losing the haunches as the horse straightens itself halfway down the long side.
Confusing haunches-in with leg yield — in travers, the horse bends toward the direction of travel.
variations: Start with haunches-in at the walk to establish the feel of the movement.
Progress from shoulder-in directly to haunches-in on the long side for a seamless transition.
Use haunches-in as preparation for half-pass: the body position is identical.
tips: Tip: Haunches-in is the mirror of shoulder-in. In shoulder-in, the shoulders come in; in haunches-in, the haunches come in. The bend always follows the direction of travel in travers.

title: Renvers (Haunches-Out)
steps: Begin at the collected trot, tracking left on the long side.
Instead of bringing the haunches in (travers), you will move the haunches to the outside — toward the rail — while the shoulders move slightly inward.
Your inside leg at the girth creates the bend. Your outside leg behind the girth is passive, allowing the haunches to swing outward.
The horse is bent in the direction of travel, just like travers, but the position relative to the rail is reversed.
Maintain the bend and angle for the entire long side. The horse should be on four tracks.
Straighten before the corner and ride forward for several strides.
Practice on both reins. Renvers is the hardest lateral movement to coordinate, so be patient.
mistakes: Confusing renvers with shoulder-in — in renvers, the horse bends toward the direction of travel.
The horse losing balance because the haunches drift too far to the outside.
Over-using the inside rein to create bend instead of using the seat and inside leg.
Not maintaining enough collection, causing the horse to fall onto the forehand.
variations: Chain shoulder-in to renvers on the same long side for a bend-change exercise.
Use renvers to prepare for half-pass toward the center line.
Practice renvers on a circle for advanced control of the haunches.
tips: Tip: Think of renvers as travers on the other side of the arena. The horse's body position is identical; only the relationship to the wall changes.

title: Half-Pass at the Trot
steps: Start at the collected trot, tracking left. At the beginning of the long side, ride shoulder-in for 3-4 strides to establish the bend.
From the shoulder-in position, shift your weight slightly to the right and ask the horse to move diagonally across the arena.
The horse should be bent in the direction of travel (left bend for left half-pass), moving forward and sideways simultaneously.
Your inside (left) leg at the girth maintains impulsion and bend. Your outside (right) leg behind the girth pushes the horse sideways.
The forehand should slightly lead the haunches. If the haunches lead, add more shoulder-fore angle.
Aim for a diagonal line from H to X or from one long side to the center line.
Straighten at your target point and ride forward for several strides before attempting the return half-pass.
mistakes: The haunches leading the shoulders, creating a crooked half-pass.
Losing the bend in the direction of travel — the horse should look where it's going.
The tempo slowing dramatically because the rider blocks forward movement with too much rein.
Tension in the horse's back causing short, tight steps instead of flowing sideways movement.
variations: Start with a shallow half-pass (small angle) and gradually increase the steepness.
Ride half-pass left to X, then straighten and half-pass right back to the track (zigzag).
Progress to half-pass at the collected canter for upper-level work.
tips: Tip: Begin every half-pass from a shoulder-in position. If the quality deteriorates, return to shoulder-in to re-establish the bend and balance.

title: Leg Yield Zigzag
steps: Place cones at the quarter line and three-quarter line on both long sides to mark your targets.
Starting on the center line at the working trot, leg yield to the right toward the three-quarter line.
When you reach the three-quarter line, change the flexion and leg yield back to the left, crossing the center line to the quarter line.
Continue zigzagging: quarter line to three-quarter line and back.
Each leg yield segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
Focus on smooth transitions between directions. The change of flexion should happen in 2-3 straight strides.
Complete 4-6 zigzag segments, then walk and stretch.
mistakes: The segments becoming uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
Not allowing enough straight strides between direction changes.
The horse becoming tense or resistant from the constant directional changes.
Losing forward energy as the zigzag progresses.
variations: Start with just 2 direction changes (one zigzag) and build up to 6.
Ride the zigzag on the center line instead of the quarter lines for more room.
Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected trot for upper-level work.
tips: Tip: Quality over quantity. Two clean direction changes are better than six sloppy ones.

title: Turn on the Forehand
steps: Halt your horse on the center line or along the rail. Establish a square, balanced halt.
To turn on the forehand to the right, apply your left leg behind the girth while keeping a steady contact.
The horse's front legs should remain almost stationary, stepping in a small circle, while the hind legs cross over and around.
Your right leg stays passive near the girth to prevent the horse from walking forward.
Your reins keep a light, even contact to prevent forward movement without pulling backward.
Ask for one step at a time. Reward each correct step before asking for the next.
Complete a 180-degree turn (half-turn on the forehand), then walk forward. Repeat in the other direction.
mistakes: The horse walking forward through the turn instead of pivoting on the front legs.
Using too much rein and causing the horse to step backward.
Asking for too many steps at once, causing the horse to rush and lose coordination.
The front legs walking a large circle instead of staying nearly in place.
variations: Progress to a full 360-degree turn on the forehand.
Practice from the halt and from the walk for different levels of difficulty.
Use the turn on the forehand as a gate-opening exercise: approach a gate, turn on the forehand to position yourself parallel to it.
tips: Tip: This is a building block for all lateral work. If your horse understands moving away from leg pressure, every lateral movement becomes easier.

title: Turn on the Haunches at Walk
steps: Collect the walk to a slow, rhythmic pace. Half-halt to lighten the forehand.
To turn on the haunches to the right, apply your left rein to guide the shoulders right while your right leg supports at the girth.
The hind legs should remain nearly stationary, stepping in a very small circle, while the front legs walk around them.
Your left leg behind the girth prevents the haunches from swinging out. Your seat stays centered.
Ask for a quarter-turn first (90 degrees). Halt, praise, and assess the quality before continuing.
Progress to a half-turn (180 degrees) and eventually a full turn (360 degrees).
The walk rhythm should be maintained throughout — the hind legs keep the walk tempo even while nearly in place.
mistakes: The haunches swinging wide instead of staying in place.
The horse stepping backward instead of turning.
Losing the walk rhythm — the horse freezing or shuffling.
Using too much inside rein, pulling the horse around instead of pushing from the outside aids.
variations: Practice the turn on the haunches from a halt for a simpler starting position.
Use the turn as a pirouette preparation: maintain walk rhythm, decrease the turn radius over time.
Chain turns on the haunches with shoulder-in departures for a seamless lateral exercise.
tips: Tip: The turn on the haunches is a baby pirouette. The key is maintaining the walk tempo in the hind legs — they should keep marching even while pivoting.

title: Shoulder-In to Circle
steps: Begin in shoulder-in at the trot along the long side.
At the midpoint of the long side (E or B), use the shoulder-in bend to flow into a 10-meter circle.
The circle should feel like a natural extension of the shoulder-in — the bend is already established.
Complete the 10-meter circle and exit back into shoulder-in on the long side.
Continue the shoulder-in to the corner, then ride the short side normally.
Repeat on the other long side. The transition from lateral movement to circle should be seamless.
This exercise develops the connection between lateral work and figures, a key skill for dressage tests.
mistakes: Losing the bend when transitioning from shoulder-in to the circle.
The circle being too large because the shoulder-in angle was too shallow.
Breaking the rhythm at the transition points.
The horse falling in on the circle because it was already on the inside track from shoulder-in.
variations: Exit the circle into haunches-in instead of shoulder-in for a bend-change exercise.
Add a transition to walk on the circle, then pick up trot for the shoulder-in exit.
Chain: shoulder-in, 10-meter circle, shoulder-in, 10-meter circle all along the long side.
tips: Tip: The shoulder-in and circle share the same bend. If your shoulder-in is correct, the circle should feel effortless.

title: Leg Yield from Center Line to Wall
steps: Start at A on the center line at the working trot.
Ride straight for 3-4 strides to establish rhythm and straightness on the center line.
Apply your left leg at the girth to leg yield to the right, moving the horse sideways toward the right wall.
Keep a slight left flexion (away from the direction of sideways travel). The horse's body should be nearly straight.
Maintain the forward tempo. The horse should reach the rail by the time you arrive at B or E.
Walk, change direction, and repeat from the center line to the other wall.
mistakes: Starting the leg yield before establishing straightness on the center line.
Too much neck bend, causing the shoulder to lead excessively.
The horse not reaching the wall, indicating insufficient lateral response to the leg aid.
Losing forward impulsion so the horse shuffles sideways without marching forward.
variations: Vary where you start the leg yield: start at C for a longer lateral distance.
Add a circle at B or E before completing the leg yield to the wall.
Increase speed to medium trot while maintaining the leg yield angle.
tips: Tip: Start with just a few sideways steps and build up. Three good steps sideways are better than ten crooked ones.

title: Shoulder-Fore at the Trot
steps: Shoulder-fore is a mild version of shoulder-in with less angle — the horse is on two tracks instead of three.
At the working trot, track right along the long side. Ask for a very slight inside flexion.
Bring the inside shoulder just barely off the track — about 2-3 inches inward. This is far less angle than shoulder-in.
Your inside leg at the girth maintains impulsion. Your outside rein controls the degree of angle.
The purpose is to align the horse's inside hind leg to step toward the outside front leg, improving straightness.
Maintain shoulder-fore for the entire long side. The horse should feel slightly more engaged behind.
Ride forward out of shoulder-fore into a straight line and notice how the horse is more balanced.
mistakes: Creating too much angle and accidentally riding shoulder-in instead of shoulder-fore.
Only bending the neck without moving the shoulder, creating neck-flexion without body alignment.
Losing the forward tempo because of too much focus on the lateral positioning.
Not being able to feel the difference between shoulder-fore and riding straight.
variations: Use shoulder-fore as a warm-up tool before every ride to improve straightness.
Apply shoulder-fore on circles to prevent the horse from falling in.
Progress from shoulder-fore to shoulder-in by gradually increasing the angle.
tips: Tip: Shoulder-fore is the most useful position in riding. It straightens your horse by aligning the hind legs with the front legs on every line.

title: Walk-Trot Transitions on the Long Side
steps: Track right at the working walk along the long side.
At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working trot.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Trot to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to walk.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the trot rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate walk and trot at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.
mistakes: The transition happening too late because the rider doesn't prepare early enough.
The trot departure being abrupt and fast instead of flowing forward smoothly.
The walk transition taking too many strides — the horse leaking forward instead of responding promptly.
The rider using too much rein for the downward transition instead of the seat and core.
variations: Add halt transitions between the walk-trot transitions for three-gait work.
Progress to trot-canter transitions at the letters.
Perform the transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of the long side.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transition 3 strides before the letter. Think of it as: prepare, ask, allow.

title: Trot-Canter Transitions
steps: Establish a balanced working trot on a 20-meter circle.
In a corner or at a circle marker, prepare for the canter transition: sit the trot for 2-3 strides.
Apply your outside leg behind the girth and your inside leg at the grit. Give a small half-halt, then allow the canter.
The first canter stride should be forward and balanced, not running or lurching.
Canter for half the circle, then transition back to trot. Sit the trot for 3-4 strides before rising.
Repeat the transition 4-6 times, then change direction and work the other lead.
mistakes: The horse picking up the wrong lead because the outside leg wasn't clear enough.
The transition being rough — the horse running into the canter instead of stepping up into it.
The rider throwing their body forward in the depart, unbalancing the horse.
Not sitting the trot before the transition, making the canter aid unclear.
variations: Practice trot-canter transitions on the long side at a specific letter.
Add walk-canter transitions for a more advanced departure.
Perform the canter depart from a 10-meter circle for improved engagement.
tips: Tip: The canter depart should feel like the horse lifts and opens the stride. If it feels like speeding up, you need more half-halt before the aid.

title: Canter-Trot-Canter Transitions
steps: Establish a working canter on a 20-meter circle.
At a designated marker, transition to trot. Allow only 3-5 trot strides.
Before the horse settles into the trot, ask for canter again with your outside leg.
The goal is prompt, clean transitions with minimal trot strides between canter segments.
Repeat 4-5 times on one lead, then change direction and work the other lead.
Gradually reduce the number of trot strides: aim for 3, then 2, then eventually 1.
This exercise builds the horse's responsiveness and engagement of the hind legs.
mistakes: Too many trot strides between canters, losing the exercise's purpose.
The horse anticipating and either not transitioning down or rushing back to canter.
Wrong lead on the second canter depart because of hurried aids.
The transitions becoming choppy and tense instead of smooth and flowing.
variations: Progress to canter-walk-canter transitions for collection work.
Perform the transitions on the long side at specific letters.
Reduce to 1 trot stride as preparation for flying changes.
tips: Tip: Think of the trot strides as a brief interruption, not a full gait change. The canter energy should stay alive in the trot moment.

title: Walk-Canter Transitions
steps: Establish a collected, active walk on a 20-meter circle. The walk must have energy and purpose.
Prepare the canter depart: half-halt, sit deep, outside leg back, inside leg at the girth.
Ask for the canter from the walk without trot strides in between. The horse should step directly into canter.
If the horse trots first, return to walk and try again. Don't accept trot strides.
Canter half the circle, then walk directly (no trot) for the other half.
Repeat 4-6 times. Each attempt should improve as the horse understands the exercise.
This is preparation for simple changes and eventually flying changes.
mistakes: Accepting trot strides in the transition instead of insisting on a clean walk-to-canter.
The walk losing energy before the depart, making the transition sluggish.
The horse jumping into canter in a rushed, unbalanced way.
Using too strong an aid, causing the horse to become tense and tight in the back.
variations: Practice the walk-canter depart on a straight line for a different challenge.
Add canter-walk transitions to complete the simple change exercise.
Perform walk-canter at specific letters for precision training.
tips: Tip: The quality of the walk determines the quality of the canter depart. If the walk is lazy, the depart will be messy.

title: Simple Change Through Walk
steps: Begin at the working canter, tracking right on a 20-meter circle.
At X (or a designated point), transition from canter to walk. Allow 3-5 clear walk steps.
After the walk steps, pick up the left lead canter — the new inside lead.
Ride the 20-meter circle on the new lead for one full revolution.
At X again, walk for 3-5 steps and pick up the right lead.
Repeat the simple change 4-6 times. Gradually reduce the number of walk steps to 3.
The walk steps should be clear, forward, and balanced — not shuffling or rushing.
mistakes: The horse trotting instead of walking between canter leads.
Too many walk strides, losing the connection between the two canters.
Picking up the wrong lead on the second canter depart.
The horse becoming tense and anticipating the lead change.
variations: Perform simple changes on a figure-eight, changing lead at X.
Reduce to 2-3 walk strides for a more advanced simple change.
Use simple changes as preparation for flying changes by progressive reduction of walk strides.
tips: Tip: Count the walk steps out loud: 'one, two, three, canter.' This keeps you disciplined about the number of steps.

title: Halt-Trot Transitions
steps: At the working trot, ride a 20-meter circle. Choose a specific point (cone or letter) for your transition.
At your marker, transition directly to halt — no walk strides in between.
The halt should be square and balanced. The horse should stand quietly for 3 seconds.
From the halt, push directly into trot. Again, no walk strides.
The trot should come forward and balanced, not rushed or running.
Repeat 4-6 times around the circle. Each transition should be cleaner than the last.
mistakes: Walk strides creeping into the transitions (either direction).
The halt being crooked, with the horse's body angled instead of straight.
The horse anticipating and halting before the marker.
The trot depart from halt being explosive instead of calm and forward.
variations: Progress to halt-canter transitions for a more advanced engagement exercise.
Perform halt-trot transitions on the center line for test preparation.
Add rein-back before the trot depart: halt, rein-back 3 steps, trot.
tips: Tip: The quality of the halt predicts the quality of the depart. Take the time to establish a good halt before asking for trot.

title: Progressive Transitions
steps: Start at the halt on the center line. Prepare mentally for a series of upward transitions.
Ask for walk. Walk for 8-10 strides with energy and rhythm.
Ask for trot. Trot for 8-10 strides with a steady tempo.
Ask for canter. Canter for 8-10 strides on the correct lead.
Now reverse: canter to trot (8-10 strides), trot to walk (8-10 strides), walk to halt.
The entire sequence should feel like a smooth escalator — each gait builds on the last.
mistakes: Rushing the upward transitions so they feel abrupt and unbalanced.
The horse breaking to trot during the canter phase because of insecure aids.
Downward transitions that take too long (dribbling through 15 strides instead of 8-10).
Losing rhythm or straightness during any of the transitions.
variations: Reduce to 5 strides per gait for a more advanced version.
Add halt-trot and halt-canter transitions to skip gaits.
Perform the progressive transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of a straight line.
tips: Tip: This exercise is like playing musical scales. The goal is evenness and flow between each gait.

title: Transition at Every Letter
steps: Using the arena letters as markers, plan a transition at every letter around the perimeter.
A to K: walk. K to E: trot. E to H: walk. H to C: trot. And so on.
The transitions must happen exactly at the letter — not before, not after.
This requires precise planning. Begin preparing 3-4 strides before each letter.
After mastering walk-trot, upgrade to trot-canter transitions at the letters.
Focus on the quality of each transition rather than the speed of moving between them.
mistakes: Transitions happening early because the rider rushes the preparation.
Transitions happening late because the horse doesn't respond promptly.
Losing count of which transition comes next and getting confused.
The horse anticipating and becoming tense as it approaches each letter.
variations: Randomly vary which transitions happen at which letters for unpredictability.
Add halt transitions at some letters for three-gait work.
Perform the exercise with eyes closed for 2-3 strides to test your feel for the horse's position.
tips: Tip: Plan your ride before you start. Know which transition happens at which letter. Riding with a mental map makes your training deliberate.

title: Medium Trot Across the Diagonal
steps: Establish a balanced working trot. Prepare for the diagonal line.
At the corner letter, turn across the diagonal. In the first 3-4 strides, ask the horse to lengthen into medium trot.
Allow the stride to grow longer and more ground-covering. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length increases.
Your posting should become bigger and more forward to match the longer stride.
Maintain the medium trot across the diagonal until 3-4 strides before the track.
Collect back to working trot before reaching the track. The collection should be smooth, not abrupt.
Change direction and repeat on the other diagonal.
mistakes: The horse running faster instead of lengthening the stride.
Losing balance and falling onto the forehand during the medium trot.
Not collecting soon enough and arriving at the track unbalanced.
The rider gripping with the knees and blocking the horse's shoulder movement.
variations: Perform medium trot on the long side for a different spatial challenge.
Add a 10-meter circle at X in the working trot before continuing medium trot.
Progress to extended trot across the diagonal for even greater lengthening.
tips: Tip: Medium trot should feel like the horse is pushing more from behind, not pulling from the front. The engine is in the hindquarters.

title: Medium Canter on the Long Side
steps: Establish a balanced working canter on the track. Sit deep and maintain a steady rhythm.
At the beginning of the long side, allow the canter to open up into medium canter.
Your hips follow the bigger canter stride. Soften your hands forward slightly to allow the horse to lengthen the frame.
The medium canter should cover more ground per stride without speeding up the tempo.
Maintain medium canter for the length of the long side.
Before the corner, collect back to working canter using half-halts from your core and outside rein.
The transition back to working canter should happen over 3-4 strides, not abruptly.
mistakes: The horse flattening and running instead of truly lengthening the canter stride.
Not collecting soon enough, causing a scrambled turn through the corner.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm in the medium — it becomes four-beat.
Leaning back too much in the collection, pulling on the horse's mouth.
variations: Perform medium canter across the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Alternate medium and collected canter every half of the long side.
Progress to extended canter for maximum ground-coverage.
tips: Tip: Think of medium canter as turning up the volume on a speaker — same song, just bigger. The rhythm doesn't change.

title: Working to Collected Trot Transitions
steps: At the working trot, ride a 20-meter circle. Establish a steady, rhythmic tempo.
At a designated point, half-halt firmly and ask the horse to shorten the stride without losing energy.
The collected trot should feel like the horse is sitting more on the haunches, with shorter but higher steps.
Maintain collected trot for half the circle, then allow the horse back to working trot.
The transition between working and collected should be gradual — think of a dimmer switch, not an on-off switch.
Repeat the transitions 4-5 times, then change direction.
mistakes: The horse simply slowing down instead of truly collecting (shorter, higher steps).
Losing impulsion in the collection — the horse becomes lazy instead of more engaged.
Over-collecting so the horse becomes tight and tense in the back.
Not allowing enough return to working trot between collection phases.
variations: Progress to extended trot from collected trot for the full range of adjustability.
Perform the transitions on the long side at specific letters.
Add collected trot to walk transitions for further collection work.
tips: Tip: Collection is not slow. It is the same energy in a shorter stride. Think of compressing a spring — same force, smaller space.

title: Lengthening Strides at the Trot
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 4.5 feet apart (normal trot stride distance).
Trot through the poles at a working trot. Feel the rhythm over the evenly-spaced poles.
Move the poles to 5 feet apart. Trot through again, allowing a slightly longer stride.
Move the poles to 5.5 feet apart. Now the horse must genuinely lengthen to reach each pole.
Focus on maintaining rhythm while covering more ground. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length changes.
Return to the 4.5-foot spacing and notice how the working trot feels more compact.
mistakes: The horse rushing and getting quick instead of lengthening the stride.
Tripping over poles because the horse is not paying attention to foot placement.
Losing balance after the poles because the lengthening threw the horse onto the forehand.
Spacing the poles too far apart, causing the horse to canter instead of trot.
variations: Use the same concept with canter poles (9-10 foot spacing, gradually increasing to 11-12 feet).
Ride the lengthening exercise without poles on the diagonal.
Add a collection exercise after the lengthening: lengthen down the long side, collect through the corner.
tips: Tip: Poles teach lengthening better than any other tool because the horse can see and feel the target stride length.

title: Collected Walk Development
steps: Start at a free walk on a long rein. Allow the horse to stretch and swing through the back.
Gradually take up the reins to a medium walk. The steps should shorten slightly but maintain energy.
Now ask for collected walk: half-halt with your seat and outside rein, asking the horse to shorten the stride further.
In collected walk, the horse should step higher and shorter, with clear four-beat rhythm maintained.
The hind legs should step under the horse's body, not trailing behind.
Maintain collected walk for 10-15 strides, then return to medium walk.
Repeat 3-4 times. Collected walk is demanding — don't overdo it.
mistakes: The walk losing its four-beat rhythm and becoming lateral (pacing).
The horse becoming tight and tense, shortening the stride by restriction rather than collection.
Over-collecting until the horse almost halts.
Not releasing back to medium walk soon enough, causing frustration.
variations: Use collected walk as preparation for walk pirouettes.
Alternate collected and extended walk every 10 strides.
Perform collected walk on a 10-meter circle to combine collection with bend.
tips: Tip: The walk is the most easily ruined gait. If the four-beat rhythm is compromised, immediately return to free walk.

title: Extended Trot Development
steps: Begin with a working trot, then collect for 8-10 strides to store energy in the hindquarters.
At the beginning of the diagonal, release that stored energy: allow the horse to push powerfully from behind.
The extended trot should show maximum ground cover with each stride. The legs should reach forward, not just up.
Your posting becomes bigger and slower to match the longer stride. Let your hands follow the head motion.
Maintain the extension across the full diagonal, keeping the rhythm steady.
Before the track, collect firmly over 4-5 strides. Don't slam on the brakes.
Walk on a long rein and assess: did the horse push more from behind or just run faster?
mistakes: Confusing extension with speed — the horse should lengthen the stride, not quicken the tempo.
Falling onto the forehand because the horse is pushing flat instead of uphill.
Losing the collection at the end, arriving at the track unbalanced.
Tension in the horse's back causing short, tight steps instead of sweeping extensions.
variations: Practice half-arena extensions: lengthen for half the diagonal, collect for the other half.
Compare your extension left vs. right — most horses extend better in one direction.
Video yourself to check if the diagonal pairs of legs reach equally (true extension) or if one pair is lazy.
tips: Tip: True extension comes from collection. You cannot extend what you haven't first compressed. Always collect before you extend.

title: Rein-Back to Trot Transition
steps: Halt your horse on the center line. Establish a square, balanced halt.
Ask for rein-back by applying both legs slightly behind the girth while keeping a steady contact that prevents forward motion.
The horse should step backward in clear diagonal pairs. Count 4-5 steps backward.
Without halting after the rein-back, immediately push the horse forward into trot.
The trot should be energetic and forward. The rein-back coils the spring; the trot releases it.
The transition from rein-back to trot tests whether the horse is truly sitting on the haunches.
mistakes: The horse dragging its feet backward instead of stepping clearly.
Halting between the rein-back and the trot, losing the energy.
Using too much rein and not enough leg to initiate the rein-back.
The trot depart being sluggish because the rein-back killed the horse's forward desire.
variations: Rein-back to canter for an even more engaged transition.
Increase to 6-8 rein-back steps before the trot.
Alternate rein-back with forward walk steps: back 3, forward 3, back 3, trot.
tips: Tip: Rein-back should feel like the horse is walking backward with energy, not being pulled backward. The legs should lift and step.

title: Center Line Riding
steps: At A, turn onto the center line heading toward C. Look straight ahead at C to guide your line.
Your horse should be absolutely straight on the center line. Use both legs evenly to prevent drift.
Check your straightness by glancing down at the line in the footing (if visible) or at the arena walls to ensure equal distance on both sides.
Ride the full center line at the walk. At C, turn left or right onto the track.
Repeat at the trot. Straightness becomes harder at faster gaits because any imbalance is magnified.
Practice the center line 4-6 times. This is a fundamental skill for every dressage test entry.
mistakes: Drifting to one side because the horse favors one shoulder over the other.
Looking down at the horse's neck instead of ahead at C.
The initial turn onto the center line being too shallow or too deep.
Losing straightness in the last 3-4 strides before C because the horse anticipates the turn.
variations: Add a halt at X for a halt-salute test preparation exercise.
Ride the center line in medium trot for added difficulty.
Practice the center line at the canter with a specific lead for test preparation.
tips: Tip: The center line reveals the truth about your horse's straightness. Film it from C and you'll see every drift and wobble.

title: Quarter-Line Riding
steps: The quarter line is 5 meters from the rail. Place cones at each end to mark the line.
Turn off the track and ride down the quarter line at the trot.
Without the rail to guide you, your horse's natural drift becomes apparent.
Use equal leg pressure to keep the horse straight. If it drifts toward the rail, apply inside leg. If it drifts inward, apply outside leg.
Ride the quarter line in both directions on both reins.
This exercise develops rider independence — you can't rely on the wall for straightness.
mistakes: The horse consistently drifting toward the rail for security.
Over-correcting and zigzagging instead of riding a straight line.
Not riding deep enough into the corner before turning onto the quarter line.
Losing the rhythm because you're focused on straightness.
variations: Ride the quarter line at the canter for increased difficulty.
Add transitions on the quarter line: trot-walk-trot without drifting.
Ride shoulder-fore on the quarter line to practice lateral positioning away from the rail.
tips: Tip: If your horse drifts toward the rail, it's telling you which shoulder is dominant. Use this information to plan your training.

title: Figure-Eight with Simple Changes
steps: Set up a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles meeting at X.
Begin at the canter on the right lead on the right circle.
As you approach X, transition to walk for 3-5 steps.
Pick up the left lead and ride the left circle at the canter.
After one full circle, return to X, walk for 3-5 steps, and pick up the right lead again.
Repeat 4-6 times. The walk steps should be clean and forward, not shuffling.
This is the fundamental simple change exercise used in Second Level dressage tests.
mistakes: Trotting instead of walking between lead changes.
Too many walk steps, losing the connection between canters.
Picking up the wrong lead after the walk transition.
The figure-eight becoming lopsided with one circle larger than the other.
variations: Reduce walk steps to 2-3 for a more advanced version.
Progress toward flying changes by eventually eliminating the walk steps entirely.
Use 15-meter circles instead of 20 for increased collection demand.
tips: Tip: Think of the walk steps as a brief rest stop, not a destination. The canter energy should stay alive through the walk.
